2004-12-01.title: World Briefing | Americas: Colombia: 2nd Safe Haven
2004-12-01.abstract: Colombian Pres Alvaro Uribe's government establishes second safe haven in Tibu to facilitate disarmament of paramilitary forces; right-wing guerrillas are sheilded from arrest or extradition (S)

2004-12-01.title: Luxury 2004:The Lure of Asia : Amid the backwaters of Beijing, a cultural heart is beating
Allawi to Meet Sunni Leaders on Election
2004-12-01.abstract: Iraq Prime Min Ayad Allawi to meet in Jordan with recalcitrant Sunni tribal leaders to persuade them to take part in Jan 30 elections; US battle deaths in November reached at least 135, largely because of Falluja offensive; total equals number killed in April when fighting raged in several cities; photo; map; US Amb John Negroponte predicts Sunnis will participate when they realize elections are going forward as scheduled; violence continues in Mosul, Bayji and other Sunni areas, killing Iraqi civilians and wounding three US soldiers; US soldier dies after roadside bombing; five others are wounded by car bomb on Baghdad airport road (M)

2004-12-01.title: World Briefing | Asia: Pakistan: Musharraf Dual-Office Bill Signed
2004-12-01.abstract: Pakistan's Parliament passes bill that allows Pres Pervez Musharraf to retain post of army chief; law will take effect on Dec 31 (S)

2004-12-01.title: World Briefing | Europe: Italy: Nationwide Strike Over Tax Cuts
2004-12-01.abstract: Nationwide general strike to protest economic policies of Italian Prime Min Silvio Berlusconi's government results in cancellations and delays across country; tens of thousands of demonstrators march in Rome, Milan, Venice and other cities (S)

2004-12-02.title: A political lightning rod fuels EU debate : 'Mandy in Brussels'
Russian Talk on Ukraine Recalls Cold War
2004-12-02.abstract: Russian officials commenting on election crisis in Ukraine use cold war vocabulary and summon bitter visions of lost imperial ambitions and fears of Western meddling in Russia's sphere of influence; Kremlin today views Ukraine in many was as belongong to Russia; even young, successful Russians share Pres Vladimir Putin's political views of Ukraine (M)

2004-12-02.title: Germans Investigate Charges Draftees Were Abused
2004-12-02.abstract: German Defense Min Peter Struck takes steps to contain widening scandal involving accusations of abuse of conscripts in German Army, while acknowledging that some trainers are under investigation; one study recently disclosed in German newpaper calls report of abuses 'tip of the iceberg,' with almost 60 percent of male German soldiers saying they were victims of harassment, insults and humiliation during their military service (M)

2004-12-02.title: Allawi Woos Sunni Arabs to Take Part in Election
2004-12-02.abstract: Prime Min Ayad Allawi of Iraq meets in Amman, Jordan, with Iraqi exiles and tribal and religious leaders as part of campaign to coax reluctant Sunni Arabs into taking part in coming Iraqi elections; prominent Sunni Arab politicians in Iraq are calling for pushing back Jan 30 election date, saying violence in Sunni regions and calls for boycott from Sunni clerics must be addressed before vote can take place; photo (M)

2004-12-03.title: At Least 27 Iraqis Killed in Day of Heavy Violence
2004-12-03.abstract: The attacks further underscore the capital's vulnerability to insurgent violence as the January elections approach.

2004-12-03.title: Will New Mayor Sweep Away Vice? You Could Bet on It
2004-12-03.abstract: Jorge Hank Rhon, colorful new mayor of Tijuana, Mexico, has been suspected of having links to murders, money laundering for drug cartels and smuggling of wild animals; scandals seem to swirl around him, but his pledge to clean up streets of Tijuana won him votes of residents fed up with city's sordid side; photo (M)

2004-12-03.title: Mortar Fire in 5 Locations Breaks Lull in Baghdad
2004-12-03.abstract: Mortar rounds explode in five places in central Baghdad, killing two Iraqis and wounding 14; disparate attacks underscore capitol's vulnerability to insurgents as Jan elections approach; attacks coincide with continuing violence in other parts of country; in Mosul, American and Iraqi forces discover 14 bodies, including three wearing uniforms of Iraqi National Guard; this brings to at least 90 number found in and around Mosul in last two weeks, many of them Iraqi police and national guard officers killed by insurgents aiming to intimidate country's fledgling security forces; photo; map (M)

2004-12-03.title: World Briefing | Europe: Russia: Rail Link To Georgian Region Is Shut
2004-12-03.abstract: Russia, making good on threat to suspend trade with separatist Abkhazia, Georgia, over disputed presidential election, closes rail line between Moscow and Abkhazia capital Sukhumi; impoverished region relies heavily on sale of food to Russia (S)

2004-12-03.title: Company described as in compliance : Retraction on pay for Annan's son

2004-12-04.title: Europe seen vulnerable in ways U.S. is not : Al Qaeda's next target?
Ex-Cali Boss Is Sent to U.S. In Drug Case
2004-12-04.abstract: Colombian Pres Alvaro Uribe signs final order to extradite Gilberto Rodriguez Orejuela, former leader of Cali cocaine cartel, who would be most prominent cocaine kingpin sent to US to face justice (M)

2004-12-04.title: Progress Seen on Election Break in Militant Attacks on Israel
2004-12-04.abstract: Mahmoud Abbas, new leader of Palestine Liberation Organization and man expected to be elected president of Palestinian Authority, has been making progress in persuading militant groups like Hamas to hold their fire against Israeli targets, at least until after voting on Jan 9 (M)

2004-12-04.title: It Was Dec. 3, but in Kiev, New Year Began Yesterday
2004-12-04.abstract: Kiev became energized in ways no one can remember after Ukraine Supreme Court nullified results of presidential election; thousands of demonstrators who had helped to paralyze city and give opposition candidate Viktor A Yuschenko time to push his case felt they had not only won lawsuit to undo electoral fraud, but were approaching moment when Pres Leonid D Kuchma would face humiliating defeat (M)

2004-12-04.title: Dead in Iraq
2004-12-04.abstract: Defense Department confirms death of one marine in iraq

2004-12-05.title: Japan at Military Crossroads With Troops on Duty in Iraq
2004-12-05.abstract: Japanese Defense Min Yoshinori Ono is pleased with deployment of 550 Japanese non-combat troops to Samawa in Iraq; it seems likely their deployment will be extended beyond end of this mission on December 14; Japanese public is less enthusiastic about mission; critics say Samawa can no longer be considered non-combat zone, as troops cannot safely leave camp to do aid work; Japanese troops have sustained no casualties in year-long deployment, chief significance of which is as step in broader transformation of Japan's military (M)

2004-12-05.title: Insurgents' Attacks Kill at Least 26 Iraqis
2004-12-05.abstract: Insurgents carry out second day of deadly attacks in Baghdad and Mosul; roadside bomb in Baghdad kills one American soldier and wounds five others, while suicide bomber kills eight Iraqi police officers and wounds 38; insurgents shooting from mosque in Mosul kill two American soldiers and wound four others, while suicide bomber hits convoy of Kurdish militiamen, killing at least 18 and wounding 16; attacks in other areas noted; map; photo (M)

2004-12-05.title: Ukraine's Face Is Mirrored in a Candidate
2004-12-05.abstract: Ukrainian opposition presidential candidate Viktor A Yushchenko uses his own disfigured face to underscore message that things are so fundamentally ugly in Ukraine that they must fundamentally change; contends cysts marring his once-handsome face are result of poisoning by political enemies; offers hope for modern civil society in place of Pres Leonid Kuchma's what Yushenko and his supporters see as shadowy, thuggish and corrupt government, but it is unclear if he can deliver; critics say that Yushchenko has no issues that unify his coalition aside from opposition to current government; photo (M)

2004-12-05.title: Governing Party Seems to Hold Big Lead in Mozambique Ballot
2004-12-05.abstract: Preliminary results show that Frelimo Mozambique's governing party, and its candidate Armando Guebuza won about 60 percent of vote in national elections and led in 10 of 11 provinces; international observers call voting largely fair but complain about lack of access to ballot-counting process; Renamo, main opposition party has complained of gross irregularities, but it seems that support for Renamo has collapsed; Pres Joaquim Chissano is retiring after 18 years with reputation for building both democracy and economy in one of world's poorest countries (M)

2004-12-05.title: Britain Offers Health Aid for Malawi
2004-12-05.abstract: Britain pledges $100 million to raise salaries of healthworkers and increase medical training in Malawi; Malawi is in healthcare crisis due to AIDS, malaria and exodus of nurses; is seen as test case for resolving problems faced by other African nations; money increases nation's medical budget by 30 percent and is part of $200 million six-year package (M)

2004-12-06.title: Surge in Extradition of Colombia Drug Suspects to U.S.
2004-12-06.abstract: Pres Alvaro Uribe's government in Columbia has extradited more than 170 drug trafficking suspects to United States, more than combined total of other administrations since 1984; biggest prize of all was Gilberto Rodriguez Orejuela, said to be most powerful cocaine magnate ever to be extradited, who arrived in US on Dec 3; Colombian government is using these extraditions as political leverage both in its disarmament negotiations with right-wing paramilitary commanders and in its effort to prod reluctant Marxist rebel groups into negotiations; commanders on both sides are wanted in US on drug trafficking charges; Uribe's willingness to extradite has made him Pres Bush's closest ally in region marked by rising anti-Americam sentiment; photo (M)

2004-12-06.title: Gunmen Storm U.S. Post in Saudi Arabia
2004-12-06.abstract: Three attackers were killed as they raided the U.S. Consulate in Jidda. Five non-American staff members also died.

2004-12-06.title: Wave of Violence by Iraqi Rebels Kills 80 in 3 Days
2004-12-06.abstract: Series of increasingly brazen attacks by Iraqi militants leaves more than 80 people dead in past three days and deepens sense of growing mayhem as Jan elections approach; in Tikrit, militants ambush busload of unarmed Iraqi contractors employed by American forces, gunning down 17 of them; attack underscores increasing risks faced by Iraqis who work for American-led occupation and are singled out as collaborators; militants detonate car bomb in front of Shiite mosque, singling out Shiite officers for slaughter in police station attack, and killing 18 Kurdish militiamen; intensified insurgent campaign is aimed at terrorizing Iraq's fledgling security forces and fomenting sectarian divisions that could undermine elections or perhaps force delay; majority Shiite community insists that elections go forward as planned, as does Pres Bush; but political leaders, mostly Sunni Arabs representing 40 political parties and individuals, say insurgents' campaign of violence and intimidation has made credible elections impossible for moment, and that holding them in Jan would achieve illegitimate result that could provoke further civil conflict; photo (M)

2004-12-06.title: Paper birds dropped on restive provinces : Gesture of peace to south Thailand
2 Polls Show a Dead Heat in Palestinian Campaign
2004-12-06.abstract: The two leading Palestinian presidential candidates, Mahmoud Abbas and Marwan Barghouti, are in a statistical dead heat ahead of next month's election.

2004-12-07.title: World Briefing | Americas: Canada: Hearing Starts For American War Resister
2004-12-07.abstract: Three-day hearing commences in Toronto to determine if United States army deserter Jeremy Hinzman will be allowed refugee status in Canada; Hinzman holds that war in Iraq is illegal and he is refusing to serve (S)

2004-12-07.title: Libya Ties Reprieve for Nurses to Payment for AIDS Victims
2004-12-07.abstract: Libya may ''re-examine'' the death sentences handed down earlier this year to five Bulgarian nurses found guilty of infecting children at a Libyan hospital with H.I.V. in 1998, a top Libyan official has said.     But a resolution to the case remains clouded by demands that the children's families receive compensation. European medical experts have testified that the infections began before the nurses started working at the hospital.

2004-12-07.title: BBC Announces 10 Percent Cuts in Its Workforce
2004-12-07.abstract: Britain's venerable public broadcaster said 2,900 positions would be cut over three years to save around $610 million in annual costs.

2004-12-07.title: Israeli Soldier Is Killed as Palestinian Agent Sets Trap
2004-12-07.abstract: In the fighting that followed, Israeli forces killed four Palestinian militants in the bloodiest single clash since Yasir Arafat died, Hamas said.

2004-12-07.title: A Warmer Tone in Court as Milosevic Pursues His Defense
2004-12-07.abstract: After two years at Slobodan Milosevic's trial in The Hague in which the prosecution and its witnesses presented him variously as a brutal dictator and a war criminal, he is now hearing sweeter words from witnesses.     The defense for Mr. Milosevic, the former Yugoslav president and Serbian leader, has been repeatedly disrupted since it began in July, both by his health problems and legal wrangling, and has only recently resumed. In November, he won an appeal, regaining the right to lead his own defense after many of his witnesses refused to cooperate with court-appointed lawyers. Now he has called and questioned his first witnesses -- three Russians and a Serb, all former officials.

2004-12-08.title: World Briefing | Europe: Turkey: Objection To Europeans' New Conditions
2004-12-08.abstract: In a rare show of unity, Turkey's president, prime minister, army chief and opposition party leader issued a strong joint protest against European Union moves to impose new conditions for the start of membership talks. They were reacting to the latest draft statement, prepared for the European leaders' Dec. 17 summit meeting in Brussels, which reportedly criticizes Turkey's record on torture and would set tougher standards for its entry to the European bloc than for other countries. France, Austria and the Netherlands, among others, have resisted issuing Turkey an invitation to begin negotiations. Their hesitation has fueled anger across Turkey, and the Turkish leaders, clearly irritated, suggested that the Europeans were treating them unfairly.   Susan Sachs (NYT)

2004-12-08.title: World Briefing | Africa: Somalia: Warlords On Board
2004-12-08.abstract: Somalian Prime Min Mohammed Ali Geedi meets with new cabinet; National Security Min Mohammed Qanyare, who heads 2,000-member strong militia, and Interior Min Hussein Farah Aideed, son of former warlord, are members of cabinet (S)

2004-12-08.title: Iraq-Bound Troops Confront Rumsfeld Over Lack of Armor
2004-12-08.abstract: Troops complained that they were being sent into combat with insufficient protection and aging equipment.

2004-12-08.title: Islam in Jail: Europe's Neglect Breeds Angry Radicals
2004-12-08.abstract: Muslims, who make up ten percent of France's population, now account for most of prison inmates, as well as growing percentage of prison population in other European countries; photos; authorities are unsure how to address their spiritual needs while guarding against potentially toxic mix of criminal history and extremist ideology now percolating through continent; frequent result is neglect that is even greater force for radicalization; scholar Farhad Khosrokhavar says growing Muslim prison population reflects developing Islamic underclass that is increasingly sympathetic to radical ideology in countries, like France, that are slow to adjust by providing chaplains or halal food; Britain by contrast has salaried chaplains and hundreds of volunteer imams (M)

2004-12-08.title: Names of the Dead
2004-12-08.abstract: Names of Americans newly confirmed dead in Iraq; 1,270 service members have died since start of war

2004-12-09.title: Ambassador Voices U.S. Support for Annan
2004-12-09.abstract: The Bush administration said that it had faith in the U.N. secretary general and did not want to see him leave office.

2004-12-09.title: For Young Armenians, a Promised Land Without Promise
2004-12-09.abstract: Young Armenians who came of age in independent country are frustrated by lack of good jobs, situation that forces them to look abroad for work; university students, fluent in English and Russian, gather in Red Bull bar in Yerevan and discuss impatience with growing pains of their post-Soviet state and cynicism about politics; photo; map (M)

2004-12-09.title: U.S. to Give $23.5 Million for Palestinians
2004-12-09.abstract: Bush administration to provide $23.5 million in aid to Palestinian Authority to help conduct elections, establish security, meet payrolls and upgrade infrastructure in Gaza Strip (S)

2004-12-09.title: Powell Asks Europe's Help for the Sake of Iraq's People
2004-12-09.abstract: Sec Colin L Powell urges Europeans to get over their opposition to Iraq war and support Iraqi people, speech at German Marshall Fund in Brussels; he and other US official lay out range of new obligations they are asking Europeans to accept in Iraq, Afghanistan and elsewhere; Powell will attend NATO foreign ministers' meetingand says he hopes 'improving' European mood will improve more when Pres Bush visits in February (M)

2004-12-09.title: Arroyo vows aid on journalist cases

2004-12-10.title: Unicef Report Says Children in Deprivation Reach a Billion
2004-12-10.abstract: Unicef report says more than billion children worldwide suffer extreme deprivation because of war, AIDS or poverty; finds that while there have been gains in reducing death rates among young children and increasing school attendance, some of progress of last decade has been offset by AIDS and 55 civil wars since 1990; estimates that nearly half of about 3.6 million people killed in these wars were children, and almost 500,00 children died of AIDS last year; more than 640 million of world's estimated 2.2 billion children live in very poor conditions and more than 120 million, mostly girls, do not go to school; US child poverty rate fell over decade from 24.3 percent to 21.9 percent (M)

2004-12-10.title: Canada's Supreme Court Clears Way for Same-Sex Marriage Law
2004-12-10.abstract: Canadian Supreme Court says government may introduce legislation to redefine marriage nationwide as including same-sex couples, but stops short of ruling that traditional definition of marriage is unconstitutional; six provincial high courts have already found that definition discriminatory but Supreme Court ruling will give government political cover; more than 3,000 gay couples have married in Canada since Ontario court ruling in June 2003 (M)

2004-12-10.title: World Briefing | Africa: Mozambique: Opposition Claims Election Fraud
2004-12-10.abstract: Mozambique opposition presidential candidate Alfonso Dhaklama, who appears to have lost election to departing Pres Joaquim Chissano-supported candidate Armando Guebuza, says vote was fraudulent (S)

2004-12-10.title: U.S. hints at reward to a disarmed North Korea
World Briefing | South Pacific: New Zealand: Same-Sex Unions Recognized
2004-12-10.abstract: New Zealand Parliament passes legislation supported by Prime Min Helen Clark's government that gives gay partners same rights as married couples (S)

2004-12-11.title: Powell Quick to Praise Arab Forum
2004-12-11.abstract: Sec of State Colin L Powell, on his way to forum in Rabat, Morocco, on subject of bringing democracy to Arab world, says meeting is already success because 20 Arab foreign ministers are planning to attend (M)

2004-12-11.title: World Briefing | Europe: His Dimples, How Merry! His Cross -- Uh-Oh
2004-12-11.abstract: Christmas chocolates are recalled from Coudekerque-Branche schools because St Nicholas, who is etched in chocolate, is wearing mitre with cross on it; religious symbols are banned in French schools; Mayor Andre Delattre calls situation politically unhealthy (S)

2004-12-11.title: School and hospital costs : Coping with Russia's unofficial fees
Pentagon Asks Contractor to Speed Production of Armored Humvees
2004-12-11.abstract: Pentagon asks Armor Holdings, contractor building armor-protected Humvee military vehicles, to speed production to 550 vehicles month, up from its current output of 450; company says it will not be able to reach higher level until Feb or March (M)y

2004-12-12.title: South America Seeks to Fill the World's Table
2004-12-12.abstract: South America, led by Brazil, has had explosion of farm exports over past decade; growth is fueled by combination of market-friendly economic policies and advances in agronomy that have brought formerly unusable tropical lands into production and increased productivety levels beyond those in United States and Europe, challenging their traditional dominance of global farm trade; in addition to Brazil, trend is being felt in parts of Argentina, Bolivia, Paraguay and Uruguay; photos; map; graphs (L)

2004-12-12.title: For Africa's Poor, Pregnancy Is Often Life Threatening
2004-12-12.abstract: Profile of Ethiopian woman Hiwot Taddessa, who was chosen at random along with five other pregnant women around globe in World Health Organization campaign to highlight dangers of pregnancy and childbirth and huge disparities in medical care worldwide from richest to poorest countries; 17-year-old Hiwot is unmarried and, statistically speaking, in group at greatest risk; other women being tracked are in Britain, Bolivia, Egypt, India and Laos; map; photo (M)

2004-12-12.title: French Court Delays Decision on Hezbollah-Run TV Channel
2004-12-12.abstract: France's highest administrative court delays decision on banning Al Manar Arabic-language channel, run by Hezbollah, from Lebanon; government asserts that Al Manar's broadcasts are anti-Semitic and racist and support violence, but effort to shut it down has been thwarted by legal loopholes; French broadcasting regulator last month renewed license for Al Manar to operate in France as long as it abides by law; government contends it broke law four days later with inflammatory story; Al Manar is available by satellite in United States, where there are no plans to close it down, although Hezbollah has been on State Dept list of terrorist organization since 1997 (M)

2004-12-12.title: Africans Get a Cold Shoulder as Libya Turns to the West
2004-12-12.abstract: Article on plight of African migrants stranded in Libya; Col Muammar el-Qaddafi raised their hopes with talk of borderless United States of Africa, but few of estimated one million Africans who flooded into Libya found jobs; many of them have set their sights now on Europe; Libya until 2003 turned blind eye to movement of illegal immigrants, but Libya's relations with West have improved since then and Europe is eager to stop African migration to Europe; many smugglers have been arrested and getting across Mediterranean has become hazardous; map; photo (M)

2004-12-12.title: Pakistan Arrests an Afghan Militant
2004-12-12.abstract: Pakistani police in Karachi arrest Akbar Agha, leader of Taliban splinter group Jaish-e-Muslimeen; group is accused of kidnapping United Nations workers Annetta Flanigan, Shqipe Hebibi and Angelito Nayan in Kabul, Afghanistan, on Oct 28; hostages were released unharmed on Nov 23; Afghan officials insist no deal had been made, but Agha insists deal was made to release Taliban prisoners (M)

2004-12-13.title: Doctors 'clearly' see a 'third-party' role : Yushchenko poisoning suggests plot
Pentagon Weighs Use of Deception in a Broad Arena
2004-12-13.abstract: Pentagon said to be engaged in bitter, high-level debates over how far it can and should go in managing or manipulating information to influence opinion abroad; missions, if approved, could take deceptive techniques endorsed for use on battlefield to confuse adversary and adopt them for covert propaganda campaigns aimed at neutral and even allied nations; critics say such program would shatter Pentagon's credibility; question is whether Pentagon and military should undertake official program that uses disinformation to shape perceptions abroad; efforts under consideration risk blurring traditional lines between public affairs programs in Pentagon--whose charter calls for giving truthful information to media and public--and world of combat information campaigns or psychological operations; critics see proposal as Pentagon's effort to resurrect its Office of Strategic Influence, short-lived operation to provide news items, including false ones, to foreign journalists in effort to influence overseas opinion; office was closed by Defense Sec Donald H Rumsfeld under intense criticism (M)

2004-12-13.title: 15 Years After Pinochet, Chile Begins to Dismantle His Rule
2004-12-13.abstract: The authoritarian governing structure that Gen. Augusto Pinochet left behind to hamstring his civilian successors is only now being dismantled.

2004-12-13.title: Karzai Plans to Destroy Poppy Fields in 2 Years
2004-12-13.abstract: Pres Hamid Karzai says he plans to destroy Afghanistan's flourishing poppy fields and help farmers begin to grow substitute crops; says poppy production hurts economy, destroys government, and brings Afghanistan a bad name; United Nations Office on Drugs and Crime says country has become source for 87 percent of world's opium, and American military review of Afghanistan says poppy cultivation and opium production will increase there and expand influence of drug magnates at all levels of government; photo of bags of heroin and opium seized in recent raid in Afghanistan (M)

2004-12-13.title: A Sultan Looks West
2004-12-13.abstract: This week, as EU heads of state meet to decide the fate of Turkey's candidacy for EU membership, Turkish Prime Minister Recep Tayyip Erdogan will be preparing his own run for the Turkish presidency. But critics fear that Erdogan, a conservative Muslim, is moving to stamp out the country's secular policies.

2004-12-14.title: Premier Says Ex-Government Erased Data on Madrid Attack
2004-12-14.abstract: Prime Min Jose Luis Rodriguez Zapatero of Spain contends that government of his predecessor Jose Marie Aznar erased all presidential records related to Mar 11 train bombings before leaving office in Apr; aide to Zapatero says government officials worry that destruction of files might have eliminated evidence relevant to bombings; head of Aznar's party Angel Acebes says government did not erase any official documents or anything that could be useful to new government (M)

2004-12-14.title: Names of the Dead
2004-12-14.abstract: Defense Dept confirms deaths of three service members in Iraq (S)

2004-12-14.title: Schröder reaffirms his stance on Turkey
Abbas Urges Palestinians to End Armed Uprising
2004-12-14.abstract: Mahmoud Abbas has made such statements in the past, but it was his most explicit condemnation of Palestinian violence recently.

2004-12-15.title: Poison's Use as Political Tool: Ukraine Is Not Exceptional
2004-12-15.abstract: Rampant speculation continues in Ukraine about who was behind dioxin poisoning of opposition presidential candidate Viktor A Yushchenko; use of poison as sinister statecraft re-enters public arena in former Soviet republic; former KGB figure Alexander V Litvinenko recalls that poison was consider just 'ordinary tool' of trade; he and former KGB general Oleg D Kalugin both suspect Ukrainian security service, which has maintained close ties with Russian counterpart; Yushchenko fell ill Sept 5 after dinner with Ukrainian security service chief Gen Ihor P Smeshko; Western experts explain dioxin finding by Austrian doctors (M)

2004-12-15.title: On the Air, Palestinians Soften Tone on Israelis
2004-12-15.abstract: Palestinian public television modifies usual villification of Israelis and Jews in aftermath of Yasir Arafat's death, with sudden talk of reconciliation and more neutral descriptions of Israeli soldiers that parallel overall improvement in political climate; new PLO chairman Mahmoud Abbas has spoken out against incitement, although archival scenes of violence have been less frequent on television in last year as audiences wearied of gory images; analysts on both sides note more moderate tone and more talks about social and political issues after four years of violence (M)

2004-12-15.title: France bans Al Manar TV channel
Names of the Dead
2004-12-15.abstract: Names of Americans newly confirmed dead in Iraq; 1,292 American service members have died since start of war (S)

2004-12-16.title: Body of Kidnapped Turkish Worker Is Found on an Afghan Hilltop
2004-12-16.abstract: Afghan officials say that Turkish construction engineer was kidnapped and killed by suspected Islamic militants opposed to international reconstruction effort in Afghanistan; engineer was working on road project financed by United States Agency for International Development and managed by American construction company (M)

2004-12-16.title: EU ready to say 'yes' to Turkey, as well as 'but'
Stocky Monkey in Himalayas Becomes Newest Primate Species
2004-12-16.abstract: Scientists from India working in Himalayas discover new species of monkey, stocky, short-tailed, brown-haired creature they named Macaca munzala, or Arunachal macaque; discoveries of primate species unknown to science are unusual; Wildlife Conservation Society says it is not known how mamy Macaca monkeys there are, or whether they are threatened; photo (M)

2004-12-16.title: World Briefing | Europe: Spain: Madrid Bombing Victims Criticize Politicians
2004-12-16.abstract: The spokeswoman for victims of the March 11 train bombings in Madrid lashed out at the parliamentary commission investigating the attacks, saying its members had turned the proceedings into a political circus. Pilar Manjón, whose 20-year-old son was among the 191 people killed in the bombings, said the commission had been such a disappointment it should disband and a new one be created, made up of independent experts rather than politicians. She joined a growing number of critics who accuse commission members and witnesses of focusing on attacking their political opponents.   Renwick McLean (NYT)

2004-12-16.title: With Premier's Trial Over, Italians Hope for Judicial Reforms
2004-12-16.abstract: Judicial commentators in Italy voice hope for much needed reforms in country's justice system following dismissal of bribery charges against Prime Min Silvio Berlusconi; there is widespread agreement among Italy's politicians that judiciary is seriously flawed, that it moves far too slowly and that magistrates are overworked (M)

2004-12-17.title: Turks Worry That a Union With Europe Will Cost Them Their Soul
2004-12-17.abstract: Many Turks, while aware of economic benefits of joining European Union, bridle at constant flow of outside criticism and analysis that have come with their application, and at storm of public opposition in Europe; Europe has long been Turkey's obsession, firing desires of Ottoman sultans and portrayed as model of civilization, but prospect of membership also sets Turks on soul-searching analysis of where they belong and what they want; Nahit Koran, powerful hereditary landlord in ancient Anatolian heartland, expresses fear of many about loss of sovereignty, and objects to European countries with barbaric background telling Turks how to live; photos; map (M)

2004-12-17.title: At summit, haggling over the terms : For the EU, Turkey's time has arrived
Donors Consider Large Increase in Aid to Palestinians
2004-12-17.abstract: United States, Europe and Arab countries are considering greatly increasing, even doubling, aid to Palestinians on condition they and Israel take certain steps toward reducing their conflict; four-year package of $6 billion to $8 billion would be forthcoming if Palestinian elections set for Jan 9 take place successfully, if new government cracks down on militant groups and if Israel lifts scores of roadblocks and checkpoints to ease transit of goods and people in Palestinian areas; World Bank says package would be largest per person international aid program since World War II; donors believe too much of their money has gone to keep Palestinian Authority afloat, rather than to support development, but say Salam Fayyad, Palestinian finance minister, has instituted reforms improving accountability and increasing tax collections; European and Arab donors are likely to call on US to pay more attention to their views in peace talks as they demand say in return for their increased donations (M)

2004-12-17.title: Italian President Vetoes a Contested Bill for Judicial Changes
2004-12-17.abstract: Italian Pres Carlo Azeglio Ciampi, who holds largely ceremonial post, vetoes judicial reform bill passed by Prime Min Silvio Berlusconi's supporters, calling it 'clearly unconstitutional'; government says changes would make system more efficient and less political but magistrates charge attempt to stifle their independence (M)

2004-12-17.title: Membership seen as regional incentive : EU-Croatia talks may stir change in Balkans

2004-12-18.title: World Briefing | Africa: Mozambique: Election Winner Declared
2004-12-18.abstract: Belated but official results finally awarded Mozambique's presidential election to Armando Guebuza of the incumbent Frelimo Party, but opposition candidates reiterated that the vote was tainted by fraud. Final tallies showed Mr. Guebuza had captured 9 of 11 provinces to succeed his mentor, President Joaquim Chissano. But some election observers and the candidate of the opposition Renamo Party, Alfonso Dhlakama, said that a two-week delay in completing the count and suspiciously high turnouts in some provinces pointed to fraud. Mr. Dhlakama has demanded a rerun of the balloting.   Michael Wines (NYT)

2004-12-18.title: Israel Kills 8 in Gaza After Palestinian Attacks
2004-12-18.abstract: Israeli soldiers kill at least 8 Palestinians and wound 24 in Gaza Strip raid; 5 other Palestinians die when smuggling tunnel they were digging in Gaza Strip collapses; photos (M)

2004-12-18.title: Bosnian Serb Premier Quits, Criticizing West
2004-12-18.abstract: Dragan Mikerevic, leader of Bosnian Serb government, resigns to protest what he describes as unconstitutional interference in his government's affairs by country's Western administrator, Lord Ashdown; acts day after Ashdown took measures to punish Bosnian Serb authorities over their failure to locate and arrest war crimes suspects (M)

2004-12-18.title: World Briefing | Americas: Peru: Corruption Fighters To Lose Jobs
2004-12-18.abstract: The government of President Alejandro Toledo has chosen not to renew the contract of the chief of a team of special prosecutors empowered to investigate corruption, said the head of the unit, Luis Vargas. Once embraced by the government, the Ad Hoc Prosecutors unit has become a thorn for Mr. Toledo since it began investigating charges of malfeasance by the president, his wife, a presidential aide, other relatives and the government party, Peru Possible. Mr. Vargas, whose team's main focus has been on prosecuting officials in former President Alberto Fujimori's government, will finish his job on Dec. 31. A replacement has not been appointed.   Juan Forero (NYT)

2004-12-18.title: Firings on Ecuador's Top Court Stir Opposition's Wrath
2004-12-18.abstract: Ecuador's Congress, in special session called by Pres Lucio Gutierrez, recently fired 27 of Supreme Court's 31 judges and named new court; move has plunged country into uncertainty and raised questions over whether Gutierrez is trying institutional coup to consolidate power; photos (M)

2004-12-19.title: Worst Violence in Months Leaves Scores Dead in Iraq
2004-12-19.abstract: Car bombings in Najaf and Karbala killed at least 60 people and wounded about 120. In Baghdad, insurgents killed three election officials in the midst of morning traffic.

2004-12-19.title: In Northern Iraq, the Insurgency Has Two Faces, Secular and Jihad, but a Common Goal
2004-12-19.abstract: After a three-hour firefight here in northern Iraq this month, American commanders were surprised to learn that one of the 22 insurgents they had killed was a Saudi. Even more intriguing, one of the principal leaders of the insurgency attended the funeral, the commanders learned.     This was Mohammed Sharkawa, who is described as a former member of the Ansar al-Sunna organization who now directs several hundred insurgents here in Mosul. As one commander, who said Mr. Sharkawa had killed several of his own cousins, put it, he is ''a brutally ruthless criminal, almost like a mob wiseguy who started whacking dudes.''

2004-12-19.title: U.S. Waters Down Global Commitment to Curb Greenhouse Gases
2004-12-19.abstract: Two weeks of negotiations at a United Nations conference here on climate change ended early Saturday with a weak pledge to start limited, informal talks on ways to slow down global warming, after the United States blocked efforts to begin more substantive discussions.    The main focus was to discuss the Kyoto Protocol on global warming, which goes into force on Feb. 16 and will require industrial nations to make substantial cuts in their emissions of so-called greenhouse gases. But another goal had been to draw the United States, which withdrew from the accord in 2001, back into discussions about ways to mitigate climate change after 2012, when the Kyoto agreement expires.

2004-12-19.title: 15 Years After Pinochet, Chile Begins to Dismantle His Rule
2004-12-19.abstract: Gen. Augusto Pinochet, under investigation on corruption charges and increasingly feeble, is not much of a factor in Chilean politics these days. But it is only now, nearly 15 years after he was forced to leave power, that the authoritarian governing structure he left behind to hamstring his civilian successors is being dismantled.    Thanks to an agreement between the Socialist-led coalition now in office and the right-wing opposition, Chile's Congress is set to approve a constitutional reform plan that will expand civilian authority and reduce the military's ability to interfere in governing the country. The package also restores the president's power to fire military commanders and eliminates appointive Senate seats for former commanders.

2004-12-19.title: Internal Splits Emerge in Canadian Parties Over Gay Marriage
2004-12-19.abstract: A week after the Supreme Court gave the government the constitutional go-ahead to expand marriage rights to gays and lesbians, a bitter battle has emerged in Parliament that is creating deep fissures in the Liberal and Conservative parties.    Polls indicate that a comfortable majority of Canadians actively support or passively accept legislation being prepared by Prime Minister Paul Martin to redefine marriage across the country. Already courts in six provinces and one territory, all told including 85 percent of the population of nearly 32 million, have struck down old marriage laws to allow gays and lesbians to marry. Only minor protests have occurred.

2004-12-20.title: Renata Tebaldi, 82, Soprano With 'Voice of an Angel,' Dies
2004-12-20.abstract: Soprano Renata Tebaldi, one of most beloved opera singers of all time, dies at age of 82; conductor Arturo Toscanini claimed she had 'the voice of an angel'; her distinguished career recalled; her final performance was at Met, as Desdemona in Verdi's Otello in 1973; photos (L)

2004-12-20.title: Poland pegs brighter future to euro
Auction of Seized Yukos Unit Raises Suspicions
2004-12-20.abstract: Russia auctions off subsidiary of its largest oil producer, Yukos, to little-known company that registers as last-minute bidder; Gazprom, Kremlin-favored front-runner, does not even make bid; mystery surrounding winner, Baikal Finans Group, raises suspicion among industry analysts that company is front for Gazprom or state-friendly company or combination of both; Gazprom denies any connection to Baikal Finans; Yukos unit is sold to help cover more than $27 billion in tax claims that Russian government lodged against Yukos; auction seen as reminiscent of Russian privatization deals in early 1990's--murky financing, questionable bidders and unknown companies representing powerful financial groups emerging to win lucrative assets; one analyst sees Baikal Finans bid as delaying tactic to allow Gazprom more time to raise funds; Baikal Finans has won controlling stake in Yuganskneftegas unit with bid equivalent to $9.35 billion, far below fair market value of unit; photo (M)

2004-12-20.title: 'Foreign Troops Can't Do the Job'
2004-12-20.abstract: Egyptian President Hosni Mubarak, 76, thinks that peace between the Israelis and Palestinians could be nearer than we think. But the whole region is at risk. Ongoing violence in Iraq and the question of what the US will do with Iran cast a shadow over the future of the Middle East.

2004-12-21.title: President of China Warns Hong Kong's Leader to Do a Better Job
2004-12-21.abstract: Pres Hu Jintao of China publicly urges Hong Kong's leader Tung Chee-hwa to improve his management of semiautonomous Chinese territory; remark seen as rebuke; Hong Kong's many problems in sevn years since Britain returned it to Chinese rule have turned small democracy movement into important social force able to bring hundreds of thousands of people into streets (M)

2004-12-21.title: Anthony Sampson, Anatomist of Political Power, Dies at 78
2004-12-21.abstract: Anthony Sampson, British investigative journalist and author of 20 books on political and social issues, dies at age 78; photo (M)

2004-12-21.title: He warns against expecting quick exit; Rumsfeld backed as 'good, decent man' : Some Iraqi forces not ready, Bush says
Group Calls on Settlers to Resist Sharon's Plan to Evacuate Them
2004-12-21.abstract: Israel's main grouping of settlers, Yesha Council, calls for civil disobedience to resist government's plans to dismantle all Israeli settlements in Gaza Strip and four small ones in West Bank; group endorses call for nonviolent resistance, first time it has explicitly backed illegal activity in pursuit of its goals; Prime Min Ariel Sharon's government and Israeli Army say they are prepared to deal with any unrest and are prepared to seal off Gaza to prevent West Bank settlers from traveling there to help resist army and police (M)

2004-12-21.title: World Briefing | Europe: Turkey: 'Cologne Caliph' Denies Plot
2004-12-21.abstract: Metin Kaplan, a militant cleric accused of trying to overthrow the secular system and create an Islamic state, told an Istanbul court he was not a terrorist. ''I am Muslim, and a Muslim cannot be a terrorist,'' he said in a courtroom discourse sprinkled with quotes from the Koran. Turkish authorities accused Mr. Kaplan, a Turk known as the Caliph of Cologne when he lived in that German city, of organizing a failed plot to crash an airplane into the Ataturk mausoleum during an independence day ceremony in 1998. Germany extradited him in October, after Turkey abolished the death penalty. After the one-day hearing, the court adjourned until April 2005.   Susan Sachs (NYT)

2004-12-22.title: Why the Fever in Ukraine? A Few Not-So Easy Answers
2004-12-22.abstract: Various forces are credited for political upheaval and mass demonstrations in Ukraine that upset fraudulent presidential runoff election in November; Ukraine, alone among former Soviet republics, had several essential ingredients for democracy that managed to survive turmoil of 13 years of halting transition: political competition, judicial independence, and political activism of voters in vast swath of world where apathy typically rules; photos (L)

2004-12-22.title: In Paris, dignity and a meal for hungry
U.S. Cutting Food Aid Aimed at Self-Sufficiency
2004-12-22.abstract: Bush administration is reducing its contributions to global food aid programs in face of growing budget deficit growing and Pres Bush's promise to reduce spending; tells represesntatives of several charities that it is unable to honor some earlier promises and will have money to pay for food only in emergency crises like that in Darfur, in western Sudan (M)

2004-12-22.title: World Briefing | Americas: Canada: Report Endorses Muslim Law In Ontario
2004-12-22.abstract: An Ontario government report recommended that Muslims be allowed to use religious laws to settle inheritance and family disputes outside the court system. The report came after an Islamic group, the Canadian Society of Muslims, announced plans to apply Shariah, a legal code based on the Koran, to settle disputes over issues like marriage, divorce and property. The report's recommendation drew sharp criticism from several groups, including the Canadian Council of Muslim Women, which said Shariah contradicts Canadian laws of equality for women. Provincial law already gives religious authorities limited powers to arbitrate civil matters, but plans to extend those rights to include Shariah have stirred bitter debate. The report, prepared by Marion Boyd, a former Ontario attorney general and a feminist activist, also recommends safeguards to ensure that neither party in a Shariah arbitration is subject to domestic violence and that there be ample opportunities to appeal decisions.   Colin Campbell (NYT)

2004-12-22.title: U.S. General Says Mosul Blast Appears to Be Suicide Bomb
2004-12-22.abstract: "At this point, it looks like it was an improvised explosive device worn by an attacker," Gen. Richard B. Myers said.

2004-12-23.title: World Briefing | Middle East: Iran: Minister Says 'Nuclear Spies' Worked For U.S. And Israel
2004-12-23.abstract: The authorities have arrested more than 10 men over the past nine months on charges of spying on the country's nuclear activities for the United States and Israel, Intelligence Minister Ali Yunesi told IRNA news agency. He said that the men were arrested in Tehran and the southern city of Hormouzgan and that three worked for Iran's atomic energy agency. Their names will not be made public until their trial. Mr. Yunesi said that an opposition group based in Iraq, the People's Mujahedeen, had played a central role in the espionage. In 2002, the organization's military wing, the National Council of Resistance, revealed the existence of a secret nuclear facility in Natanz and a heavy-water complex near Arak. At the time, the United Nations nuclear monitoring agency was unaware of them.   Nazila Fathi (NYT)

2004-12-23.title: Foreign Team Will Watch Vote in Iraq From Jordan
2004-12-23.abstract: Representatives of seven nations meet to recruit international observers for Iraqi elections and agree to watch vote, but from safety of Amman, Jordan; say it is too dangerous to monitor voting in Iraq; specialists say international monitors are crucial tool for ensuring that difficult elections are seen as free and fair (M)

2004-12-23.title: From the bathtub to the table:Holiday carp
Rumsfeld, Under Fire, Offers Softer Side of Grief for the Dead
2004-12-23.abstract: Defense Sec Donald H Rumsfeld responds to criticism that he was insensitive to soldier's pleas for more armored equipment in Iraq and to families of troops killed there; offers impassioned defense, saying he is 'truly saddened' that anyone could think he is not working urgently to see that lives of fighting men and women are protected (M)

2004-12-23.title: World Briefing | Africa: Sudan: Aid Worker Is Killed
2004-12-23.abstract: Doctors Without Borders condemned the shooting death of one of its Sudanese relief workers in South Darfur. The worker was the second employee of the medical relief organization to be killed in the war-ravaged western region of Sudan in the last three months. The aid group said the latest killing occurred in the town of Labado during an attack led by government troops.   Marc Lacey (NYT)

2004-12-24.title: Getting Personal, Putin Voices Defiance of Critics Abroad
2004-12-24.abstract: Pres Vladimir V Putin, at annual holiday-season news conference, defends Russia's takeover of main subsidiary of Yukos oil company by state oil company, and expresses deep irritation at West's support for popular uprisings in post-Soviet states and what he describes as Western double standards on elections; remarks seen as forceful restatement of widely known positions and sometimes layered explanation of views driving Russia in increasingly autocratic direction this year; Putin is dismissive of Pres Aleksander Kwasniewski of Poland, who helped mediate impasse in Ukraine after fraudulent presidential elections there, and who recently said that Ukraine free of Russia's sway is better for world's leading countries; many Russian politicians have accused United States of underwriting and encouraging mass demonstrations in Ukraine, but Putin pointedly expresses his satisfaction with his relationship with Pres Bush; photos (M)

2004-12-24.title: 2 Very Different Americans Shared Common Goal in Iraq
2004-12-24.abstract: Thomas Dostie and Anthony M. Stramiello Jr. were separated by more than four decades in age, but their lives ended together in a mess tent in Mosul.

2004-12-24.title: Brewmaster Breaks One Tradition but Upholds Another
2004-12-24.abstract: Ringnes Brewery, Norway's biggest, has its only woman brewmaster, Olaug Flakne, in charge of 30 men producing more than 20 million gallons of beer a year; her presence reflects urge across Norwegian society to promote women in politics, ublic service and privte businesses; true test of Norwegian brewmaster is period around Christmas when breweries compete with special Christmas beers harking back to pagan days when strong ale was brewed at winter solstice; photo (M)

2004-12-25.title: Letter from America: Maybe it's not puffing that fattened the land
Fear Keeps Pews Empty in Baghdad
2004-12-25.abstract: Attendance is sparse at churches in Baghdad day before Christmas and all Christmas Eve services are canceled because of martial law curfew; photos (M)

2004-12-25.title: U.S. Can Beat Insurgents, Rumsfeld Tells Troops
2004-12-25.abstract: Defense Sec Donald Rumsfeld, in Christmas Eve visit to several hostile Iraqi regions, tells troops that Iraq 'looks bleak,' but there is no question that US will prevail over insurgents (M)

2004-12-25.title: A Mongolian and His Nation, Evolving Together
2004-12-25.abstract: Profile of Tsakhia Elbegdorj notes that he has completed his first 100 days as prime minister of Mongolia, Central Asia's only multiparty democracy; photo (M)2

2004-12-25.title: Bitter Divisions Rife in Ukraine as Voting Nears
2004-12-25.abstract: Ukraine, over month after disputed presidential election, prepares for new vote that has raised hopes that amicable end to its deeply divisive political crisis is near; Prime Min Viktor F Yanukovich, who was declared winner of election overturned by Supreme Court, says results of Dec 26 vote will be illegitimate and vows to fight them in court; polls show opposition leader Viktor A Yushchenko with comfortable lead; map; photo (M)

2004-12-26.title: Iraq Militants Post Video on Bombing of U.S. Base
2004-12-26.abstract: The authenticity of the videoÂposted on a Web site that has carried messages from rebelsÂcould not be determined.

2004-12-26.title: In Mosul, G.I.'s Pray for Their Futures, Too
2004-12-26.abstract: American soldiers here ate Christmas lunch in flak jackets and combat helmets and prayed for the dead in candlelit ceremonies, marking a somber holiday after the bomb attack on their base on Tuesday, which killed 22 people.    Like the low, gray clouds in the sky, the attack loomed over Forward Operating Base Marez, brokering an odd partnership of Christmas and conflict at mealtimes and in prayers.

2004-12-26.title: Toll From Tanker Blast Reaches 9 in Baghdad
2004-12-26.abstract: The death toll from the blast of a tanker truck in Baghdad on Christmas Eve rose to nine on Saturday, as investigators here discovered the bodies of a family under the rubble of their home, the police said.     The truck, carrying butane gas and wired with explosives, blew up after speeding down a street in the Mansour neighborhood in western Baghdad, home to many officials and foreign embassies. Witnesses said the truck appeared to be aiming for the Jordanian Embassy but detonated too far away, leveling a nearby house.

2004-12-26.title: Most Powerful Quake in 40 Years Triggers Death and Destruction
2004-12-26.abstract: The 8.9-magnitude quake triggered tsunamis that killed more than 10,000 people. Sri Lanka, India and Indonesia were hit hardest.

2004-12-26.title: Polls in Ukraine Show Lead for Yushchenko
2004-12-26.abstract: Exit polls and the campaign of the opposition challenger, Viktor A. Yushchenko, predicting an indisputable victory.

2004-12-27.title: Bush and Other Leaders Quick to Offer Condolences and Aid
2004-12-27.abstract: World's political and spiritual leaders urge support for victims of giant tidal waves in Asia as relief agencies and governments gird to offer technical help and aid; United States says assistance is already on its way to some of most stricken areas (M)

2004-12-27.title: Afghan Students Are Back, but Not the Old Textbooks
2004-12-27.abstract: Teachers College of Columbia University is writing schoolbooks for Afghanistan as it emerges from years of turmoil and ideological repression; new books will replace outdated texts produced piecemeal in turmoil of Afghanistan's last quarter century of conflict by international aid groups and rotating governments, including Communists and Taliban; project began at prodding of Barry Rosen, head of Teachers College press office who was one of hostages held in American Embassy in Tehran in 1979; photos (M)

2004-12-27.title: Israel Frees 159 Palestinians
2004-12-27.abstract: The Palestinian leadership welcomed the move while calling for the release of thousands more who remain jailed.

2004-12-27.title: At the epicenter, a perfect wave-maker was created
Yushchenko Able to Serve, but Will Need Longtime Care
2004-12-27.abstract: Doctors and foreign specialists say Viktor A Yushchenko has recovered enough from poisoning with chemical dioxin that he should be able to serve as Ukraine's president, but he will continue to need treatment for months if not years; they caution that his illness is virtually one of a kind, caused by ingesting highly potent form of dioxin called TCDD; say it is difficult to predict with great certainty course of his recovery; most of treatment he will need is somewhat experimental; photo (M)

2004-12-28.title: China Uses Defense Report to Renew Warnings to Taiwan
2004-12-28.abstract: The Chinese government said that relations with Taiwan were "grim" and vowed that it would accelerate military modernization.

2004-12-28.title: Blogs Provide Raw Details From Scene of the Disaster
2004-12-28.abstract: Web's so-called blogosphere proves ready medium for instant news of tsunami disaster and for collaboration over ways to help; editor of one blog site claims bloggers at the scene are more deeply affected by events than journalists who roam from one disaster to another; says that makes blogs compelling reading (M)

2004-12-28.title: At Warning Center, Alert for the Quake, None for a Tsunami
2004-12-28.abstract: Experts at Pacific Tsunami Warning Center in Honolulu had no way of knowing that earthquake that struck off Indonesia had generated devastating tsunami and no way to warn people most likely to suffer; sequence of events as knowledge of earthquake, tsunami and destruction unfolded suggest speed and precision of science and modern communication, as well as their limits; scientists do not have tools to tell when earthquake has created one; for Pacific Ocean, there are computer models to analyze consequences of earthquake, based on years of observations of previous quakes and tsunamis; for Indian Ocean, where tsunamis are rare, there are no such models (M)

2004-12-28.title: Costliest Natural Disasters Since 1900
2004-12-28.abstract: Following are estimated costs for natural disasters since 1900.

2004-12-28.title: Yushchenko Wins 52% of Vote; Rival Vows a Challenge
2004-12-28.abstract: Viktor A Yushchenko, opposition leader, is Ukraine's presumptive president-elect after virtually all votes are counted; leads Prime Min Viktor F Yanukovich by 52 percent to 44 percent; Yanukovich refuses to accept results and says he will challenge them in court, but he has mustered few supporters; Kiev is calm after weeks of civil disobedience, and there are signs that evident outcome of election is being recognized abroad; Secretary of State Colin L Powell says election appears free and fair; Foreign Ministry of Belarus, Ukraine's neighbor, says it respects decision of Ukrainian people; European Union foreign policy chief Javier Solana also signals his satisfaction; importantly, leaders of prominent team of international election observers declare conduct of voting was substantial improvement over previous rounds; photo (M)

2004-12-29.title: Names of the Dead
2004-12-29.abstract: Department of Defense identifies 1,319 American service members who have died since the start of the Iraq war (S)

2004-12-29.title: Bush Directly Assails bin Laden's Message to Iraqis
2004-12-29.abstract: President Bush took the unusual step of responding today to one of Osama bin Laden's tape taunting recordings.

2004-12-29.title: Sounding the Alarm on a Tsunami Is Complex and Expensive
2004-12-29.abstract: Geoscience experts say that process of issuing warnings on tsunamis is not simple; NASA Web site indicates that three of four tsunami warnings issued since 1948 have been false and that and cost of false alarms can be high; description of system that warns communities on West Coast of United States (M)

2004-12-29.title: Ignorance of EU kills nominee's chances : Romania cabinet switch

2004-12-30.title: World Briefing | Africa: Uganda: Government And Rebels In Talks
2004-12-30.abstract: Peace talks began near the Uganda-Sudan border between the Ugandan government and an elusive rebel group called the Lord's Resistance Army, which has terrorized Ugandans for the last 18 years. The rebels, led by a mystic named Joseph Kony, have reinforced their ranks over the years by kidnapping children. The group's spokesman, Sam Kolo, told the government officials and journalists present: ''We could kill you all now for nothing but that's not our aim. We are committed to peace 100 percent.''   Marc Lacey (NYT)

2004-12-30.title: On Other Side of the World, Little to Do but Offer Prayers
2004-12-30.abstract: Sweden may have suffered more than any European nation from tsunami disaster in South Asia; estimated 20,000 Swedes were in Thailand for year-end vacations, and 1,500 of them are unaccounted for; also missing are 900 from Norway, 1,000 from Germany and 1,200 from Switzerland; photo (M)

2004-12-30.title: Water Is Key to Averting Epidemics Along Coasts
2004-12-30.abstract: World Health Orgn officials say large quantities of clean water would be best medicine for tens of thousands of tsunami survivors at risk from diseases spread by dirty water, mosquitoes and crowding; say water shortages have already occurred in Maldives and Sri Lanka, and tanker trucks are needed to provide clean water; water-purifying tablets are being rushed into affected countries, along with medicines to treat dehydration that can result from diarrhea; photo (M)

2004-12-31.title: Iraq's Kurds Enjoy Self-Rule and Are Trying to Keep It
2004-12-31.abstract: Kurdish leaders are determined to preserve informal but unmistakable autonomy they now enjoy in Erbil and entire Kurdish region of northern Iraq; residents already call area Kurdistan; territory is world apart from Iraq, with building boom, new apartments, hospitals and shopping centers; Kurdish sector is calm, free of violence plaguing large areas of Iraq; tight security is maintained by swarms of Kurdish police officers and militiamen; some Iraqi leaders resent most important safeguard of Kurdish independence: power to veto new constitution; photos; map (M)

2004-12-31.title: China joins confusion surrounding Yukos unit
In a Corner of Sri Lanka, Devastation and Divisions
2004-12-31.abstract: Abdul Hamid Ansar woke up before dawn Thursday, whispered prayers, gulped down a cup of steaming tea and resumed his surreal new existence: overseer of Sri Lanka's coast of death.     Mr. Ansar, a shy, 42-year-old divisional secretary, the Sri Lankan equivalent of an American city manager, has led the search for bodies along a four-mile stretch of congested coastline where 9,000 people are believed to have been killed when a tsunami crashed in on Sunday. His small slice of Sri Lanka appears to have the highest death toll of any single community in Asia outside of Aceh Province in Indonesia.

2004-12-31.title: Many Still in Need as Aid Is Trickling to Stricken Area
2004-12-31.abstract: Human tally in Indonesia jumps to 80,000 after officials report uncovering 28,000 more bodies in Aceh Province, near epicenter of enormous undersea earthquake; food drops and other aid trickles toward region from around world, but injured and stranded in many places still have to fend for themselves; death toll in all affected countries surges past 120,000, and international health experts say at least three times that number may be seriously injured, their survival dependent on arrival of urgent medical aid; Secretary General Kofi Annan says World Bank pledged $250 million, raising total amount devoted to emergency to nearly $500 million; says more than 30 countries and millions of individuals have joined in global campaign; says World Bank, along with United States, Australia, India and Japan have formed 'core group' to lead coordinating relief drive; extent of red tape is demonstrated when United Nations Children's Fund plane with enough aid to help 200,000 people lands at Jakarta airport, and is forced to wait day for customs clearance before it could be moved to disaster scene far north; fears of looting and disease rise, and people throughout region remain jittery; Indian officials issue warning of another tsunami, sending people in India and Sri Lanka fleeing from beaches; warning appears to be false alarm; photos; map (L)

2004-12-31.title: Text Messaging Pushed for Use as Disaster Warning Systems
2004-12-31.abstract: Experts say thousands of deaths might have been avoided if warning systems had been in place to alert people around rim of Indian Ocean of tsunami; no such system exists there now; those who design and use wireless technology known as Short Message Service, currently used for chatter and advertisements, say it could be used to jumpstart governments' warning networks; text messaging is wildly popular worldwide and has accompanied international boom in cellphone use (M)
